explanations.1: The conducting zone includes structures like the pharynx, nasal cavity, and bronchi that move air; the alveoli are part of the respiratory zone where gas exchange occurs.
explanations.2: The nasal conchae serve to increase the surface area of the nasal cavity and disrupt airflow to ensure air is warmed, cleaned, and humidified.
explanations.3: The fauces is the opening at the back of the oral cavity that leads into the oropharynx, whereas the internal nares connect the nasal cavity to the nasopharynx.
explanations.4: The trachea is characterized by C-shaped hyaline cartilage for structural support, smooth muscle (trachealis) for flexibility, and a ciliated epithelium to move mucus.
explanations.5: The bronchial tree consists of the branching tubes including the bronchi and bronchioles; the alveoli are the individual air sacs at the end of the respiratory zone.
explanations.6: Alveolar macrophages are immune cells that patrol the alveoli to remove pathogens and debris, whereas surfactant is secreted by type II alveolar cells.
explanations.7: Fissures are deep folds in the lung tissue that separate the lungs into distinct lobes, while the pleura is the membrane that covers the lungs.
explanations.8: A bronchopulmonary segment is a discrete unit of lung tissue served by its own tertiary (segmental) bronchus and its own artery.
explanations.9: Pulmonary circulation transports deoxygenated blood to the lungs to pick up oxygen and release carbon dioxide, while bronchial circulation supplies the lung tissue itself.
explanations.10: The pleura consists of two layers: the visceral pleura, which covers the surface of the lung, and the parietal pleura, which lines the thoracic wall.
explanations.11: Pulmonary ventilation relies on atmospheric pressure to create the pressure gradient necessary to move air into and out of the lungs.
explanations.12: According to Boyle's Law, the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume, meaning a decrease in volume leads to an increase in pressure.
explanations.13: Transpulmonary pressure is the pressure difference between the intra-alveolar and intrapleural pressures, which helps keep the lungs inflated.
explanations.14: Gas flow is inversely proportional to resistance; therefore, as resistance in the airways increases, the flow of air into the lungs decreases.
explanations.15: Contraction of the external intercostal muscles lifts the ribs and moves the sternum upward and outward, increasing the thoracic volume during inspiration.
explanations.16: Residual volume is the amount of air remaining in the lungs after a maximal expiration, which prevents the alveoli from fully collapsing.
explanations.17: Gas moves from an area of high partial pressure to an area of low partial pressure through the process of simple diffusion down a concentration gradient.
explanations.18: When ventilation is insufficient, the low partial pressure of oxygen causes the pulmonary capillaries to constrict, redirecting blood flow to better-ventilated alveoli.
explanations.19: Internal respiration refers to the exchange of gases between the blood and the body's tissues, while external respiration occurs in the lungs between the alveoli and blood.
explanations.20: Carbon dioxide moves down its partial pressure gradient from the blood (45 mm Hg) into the alveoli (40 mm Hg) to be exhaled from the body.
explanations.21: Oxyhemoglobin is the molecule formed when oxygen molecules bind to the iron-containing heme groups of hemoglobin in red blood cells.
explanations.22: The affinity of hemoglobin for oxygen is influenced by several factors, including temperature, pH (hydrogen ion concentration), and the presence of 2,3-BPG.
explanations.23: In the chloride shift, bicarbonate ions leave the erythrocyte and enter the plasma, while chloride ions enter the erythrocyte to maintain electrical balance.
explanations.24: The Haldane effect describes how the level of oxygen saturation of hemoglobin affects its affinity for carbon dioxide, with deoxygenated blood carrying more CO2.
explanations.25: Hyperventilation is an increased ventilation rate that exceeds metabolic demand, leading to low carbon dioxide levels and an increase in blood pH.
explanations.26: Exercise increases the body's oxygen demand and utilizes the venous reserve; at high altitudes with low oxygen, this can quickly lead to symptoms of AMS.
explanations.27: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a hormone produced by the kidneys that stimulates the bone marrow to increase the production of erythrocytes (red blood cells).
explanations.28: While most of the respiratory system develops from endoderm, the olfactory pits—which eventually form the nasal cavity lining—develop from ectoderm.
explanations.29: Although gas exchange occurs at birth, the lungs continue to develop new alveoli throughout childhood until a full complement is reached around age 8.
explanations.30: Pulmonary surfactant reduces surface tension in the alveoli; without enough of it, the alveoli are prone to collapse, making it difficult for a premature infant to inflate the lungs.
explanations.31: Fetal breathing movements, which involve the inhalation and exhalation of amniotic fluid, typically begin around week 20 of gestation.
explanations.32: Immediately after birth, the remaining amniotic fluid in the lungs is rapidly absorbed into the pulmonary capillaries and the lymphatic system.
